• Highlighted India's significant contributions to renewable energy and climate resilience, despite lower per capita emissions.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
prelimsPoints: CBDR-RC is a principle enshrined in the UNFCCC (United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change).
It acknowledges that all countries have a shared responsibility to address climate change but recognizes varying capabilities and historical contributions to emissions.
Developed countries are expected to provide financial and technological support to developing countries.
India's NDCs aim for 45% reduction in emissions intensity by 2030 from 2005 levels and 50% cumulative electric power installed capacity from non-fossil fuel sources by 2030.
mainsPoints: Explain the principle of 'Common but Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities' (CBDR-RC) and its relevance in contemporary climate negotiations.
Discuss India's consistent position on climate justice and the demands for climate finance and technology transfer from developed nations.
Analyze the challenges faced by developing countries like India in balancing economic development with ambitious climate action targets.
Evaluate India's domestic climate initiatives and their role in strengthening its bargaining position on the international stage.
GS3.imageDescription: Global map with arrows indicating international cooperation on climate change.
GS3.trivia: The first Earth Summit in Rio de Janeiro in 1992 established the UNFCCC and the principle of CBDR-RC.

GS3.headline: Government Boosts National Action Plan to Combat Rising Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR)
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare announced enhanced measures under the National Action Plan on Antimicrobial Resistance (NAP-AMR).
• Focus areas include stricter regulation of antibiotic sales, promotion of rational use of antibiotics, and increased surveillance of AMR patterns.
• Aims to raise public awareness about the dangers of antibiotic misuse and promote infection prevention and control practices.
• The 'One Health' approach, integrating human, animal, and environmental health, is central to the updated strategy.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
GS3.source: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
prelimsPoints: AMR occurs when microbes (bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites) evolve to withstand antimicrobial drugs.
India launched its NAP-AMR in 2017, aligned with the WHO Global Action Plan.
The 'One Health' approach recognizes the interconnectedness of human, animal, and environmental health in tackling AMR.
Misuse and overuse of antibiotics in humans and animals are primary drivers of AMR.
Common drug-resistant infections include TB, malaria, and typhoid.
mainsPoints: Discuss the multifaceted challenges posed by Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) to global and national public health.
Analyze the key components and strategies of India's National Action Plan on AMR, particularly the 'One Health' approach.
Evaluate the socio-economic implications of rising AMR, including healthcare costs, productivity loss, and impact on sustainable development goals.
Suggest policy interventions and public awareness campaigns that can effectively curb the spread of AMR in India.
GS3.imageDescription: Microorganisms under a microscope, symbolizing the threat of antimicrobial resistance.
GS3.trivia: AMR is projected to cause 10 million deaths annually worldwide by 2050 if not addressed, surpassing cancer as a cause of mortality.

GS3.headline: Border Roads Organisation Completes Strategic All-Weather Tunnel in Ladakh
GS3.explainer:
• The Border Roads Organisation (BRO) announced the completion of a crucial all-weather tunnel in the high-altitude region of Ladakh.
• The tunnel significantly reduces travel time and ensures year-round connectivity to remote border areas, crucial for troop movement and civilian supplies.
• It is part of India's broader strategy to enhance infrastructure along its northern borders to improve strategic mobility and security.
• The project involved overcoming extreme topographical and climatic challenges, showcasing India's engineering prowess.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Infrastructure: Roads, Ports, Airports, Railways etc.
prelimsPoints: BRO is responsible for constructing and maintaining road networks in India's border areas and friendly neighboring countries.
Ladakh is a strategically important region bordering China and Pakistan.
All-weather connectivity is vital for military logistics and civilian access, especially during harsh winters.
This tunnel is part of a series of infrastructure projects including bridges and highways in border regions.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of developing all-weather infrastructure, such as tunnels and roads, in India's border regions like Ladakh.
Analyze the role of the Border Roads Organisation (BRO) in enhancing national security and promoting socio-economic development in remote areas.
Examine the engineering challenges involved in constructing infrastructure in high-altitude, seismically active zones and the technological solutions employed.
Evaluate how improved border connectivity impacts India's geopolitical standing and its ability to respond to security challenges.
GS3.imageDescription: Entrance to a newly constructed tunnel in a mountainous, snowy landscape.
GS3.trivia: The Atal Tunnel, also built by BRO, is the world's longest highway tunnel above 10,000 feet (3,000 meters).

GS3.headline: India on Track to Exceed Renewable Energy Capacity Targets, New Report Suggests
GS3.explainer:
• A new report by the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy indicates that India is well on its way to surpassing its 2030 renewable energy capacity targets.
• Rapid expansion in solar and wind power projects, coupled with supportive government policies, are driving this growth.
• The report highlighted increased private sector investment and technological advancements as key enablers.
• This progress is crucial for India's energy security, reducing carbon emissions, and achieving climate goals.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy
Environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
GS3.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy
prelimsPoints: India's target is to achieve 500 GW of non-fossil fuel energy capacity by 2030.
The National Solar Mission is a major initiative under the National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC).
Schemes like PM-KUSUM (Pradhan Mantri Kisan Urja Suraksha evam Utthaan Mahabhiyan) promote solar energy in agriculture.
India is the third-largest renewable energy market in the world.
mainsPoints: Assess India's progress in achieving its ambitious renewable energy capacity targets and the factors contributing to this success.
Discuss the economic and environmental benefits of India's transition to renewable energy sources, including energy security and climate change mitigation.
Examine the challenges that remain in scaling up renewable energy, such as grid integration, storage solutions, and land acquisition.
Evaluate the role of government policies, private investment, and technological innovation in accelerating India's renewable energy journey.
GS3.imageDescription: A vast solar power farm stretching into the horizon, with clear skies.
GS3.trivia: India has the fourth-largest installed renewable energy capacity globally.

GS3.headline: Global Food Security Index 2024: India Shows Improvement Amidst Global Challenges
GS3.explainer:
• The latest Global Food Security Index (GFSI) report showed India improving its ranking, reflecting enhanced efforts in food availability and affordability.
• Despite global challenges like climate change and geopolitical conflicts impacting food supply chains, India demonstrated resilience.
• The report acknowledged government initiatives such as the National Food Security Act and various agricultural support schemes.
• However, concerns remain regarding nutritional quality and sustainable resource management, highlighting areas for further improvement.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices
Public Distribution System—objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security
Food processing and related industries in India—scope’ and significance, location, upstream and downstream requirements, supply chain management
prelimsPoints: The GFSI is released by the Economist Intelligence Unit (EIU) and measures food security across four pillars: affordability, availability, quality and safety, and sustainability and adaptation.
The National Food Security Act (NFSA) 2013 aims to provide subsidized food grains to approximately two-thirds of India's population.
India is a major producer of several agricultural commodities.
Challenges include food waste, climate vulnerability, and hidden hunger.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's performance in the Global Food Security Index, identifying areas of improvement and persistent challenges.
Discuss the effectiveness of key government policies and programs, such as NFSA and PDS, in ensuring food availability and affordability in India.
Examine the impact of climate change and global supply chain disruptions on India's food security and agricultural sector.
Suggest comprehensive strategies to address nutritional security and promote sustainable agricultural practices for long-term food security in India.
GS3.imageDescription: A diverse array of fresh fruits and vegetables, symbolizing food availability.
GS3.trivia: India's food grain production has consistently set new records in recent years, contributing significantly to national food security.

GS3.headline: India Finalizes Monsoon Preparedness Plan for 2025, Focus on Early Warning
GS3.explainer:
• The National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) finalized its comprehensive monsoon preparedness plan for 2025, emphasizing early warning systems.
• The plan includes pre-positioning of disaster response teams, inventorying essential supplies, and upgrading communication networks.
• Special focus is given to flood-prone regions and coastal areas susceptible to cyclones, with enhanced community engagement.
• Aims to minimize loss of life and property by improving coordination among central, state, and local agencies.
gsSubTopics: Disaster and disaster management
prelimsPoints: NDMA is the apex body for disaster management in India, headed by the Prime Minister.
IMD provides weather forecasts and early warnings for monsoons and cyclones.
NDRF and SDRF are specialized forces for disaster rescue and relief operations.
Community-based disaster risk reduction is a crucial component of preparedness.
Monsoon season typically runs from June to September in India.
mainsPoints: Discuss the multi-pronged approach adopted by India's National Disaster Management Authority for monsoon preparedness, focusing on early warning and response.
Analyze the challenges in effective disaster management during the monsoon season, particularly in densely populated and vulnerable regions.
Evaluate the role of various agencies, including IMD, NDRF, and local administration, in ensuring a coordinated and timely response to monsoon-related disasters.
Suggest measures to enhance community resilience and integrate traditional knowledge with modern technology for better disaster risk reduction.
GS3.imageDescription: A rescue team evacuating people from a flooded area during monsoon.
GS3.trivia: India's monsoon rains are vital for its agricultural economy, contributing to about 70% of the annual rainfall.

GS3.headline: Digital Public Infrastructure Expansion: Addressing Cybersecurity Challenges
GS3.explainer:
• As India continues to expand its Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) like Aadhaar, UPI, and DigiLocker, cybersecurity concerns take center stage.
• Experts highlighted the need for robust security frameworks, regular audits, and public awareness campaigns to protect user data and systems.
• The government outlined plans to invest further in AI-driven cybersecurity solutions and enhance collaboration with private sector security firms.
• Ensuring trust and resilience in DPI is crucial for sustained digital transformation and economic growth.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Basics of cyber security; role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
prelimsPoints: DPI refers to shared digital systems that are foundational for public and private service delivery.
Aadhaar is a 12-digit unique identification number.
UPI is a real-time payment system developed by NPCI (National Payments Corporation of India).
DigiLocker provides a secure cloud-based platform for issuance and verification of documents.
CERT-In (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team) is the national agency for cybersecurity incident response.
mainsPoints: Discuss the transformative potential of India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) in driving financial inclusion, governance, and economic growth.
Analyze the major cybersecurity threats and vulnerabilities associated with the widespread adoption and expansion of DPI.
Evaluate the measures being taken by the government and other stakeholders to enhance the security and resilience of DPI.
Examine the role of international cooperation and public-private partnerships in building a robust cybersecurity ecosystem for DPI.
GS3.imageDescription: Interconnected digital icons representing various DPI services like payments, identity, and education.
GS3.trivia: India's UPI system processes more transactions than credit cards and debit cards combined in many developed countries.

GS3.headline: India's First Private Sector Liquid Rocket Engine Successfully Test-Fired
GS3.explainer:
• A significant milestone was achieved in India's private space sector with the successful test-firing of a liquid rocket engine developed by a private startup.
• This marks a crucial step towards indigenous private space launch capabilities and reduced reliance on foreign technology.
• The test demonstrated the potential for cost-effective and agile space missions from India.
• It aligns with the government's reforms to open up the space sector to private players, fostering innovation and competition.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
prelimsPoints: This is a key outcome of India's space sector reforms announced in 2020.
IN-SPACe (Indian National Space Promotion and Authorization Centre) acts as a single-window agency for private entities.
NSIL is the commercial arm of ISRO.
Liquid rocket engines offer thrust vector control and precise control over burn time.
India aims to become a global hub for space technology and services.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of private sector participation in India's space program and its potential impact on innovation, cost-effectiveness, and global competitiveness.
Analyze the role of government policies and regulatory bodies like IN-SPACe in fostering a thriving private space ecosystem in India.
Evaluate the technological challenges and opportunities associated with developing indigenous rocket engines and launch vehicles by private players.
Examine how a robust private space sector can contribute to India's strategic autonomy and economic growth.
GS3.imageDescription: A powerful liquid rocket engine undergoing a test-fire, with flames emanating.
GS3.trivia: India is one of the few countries with the capability to launch its own satellites and those of other nations.

GS3.headline: Ministry of Environment Issues New Guidelines for Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change released updated guidelines for Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) processes.
• The new guidelines aim to streamline the clearance process while strengthening environmental protection mechanisms.
• Key changes include mandatory public hearings for certain projects, enhanced post-clearance compliance monitoring, and clearer definitions of project categories.
• Seeks to balance industrial development with ecological sustainability and ensure greater transparency.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
GS3.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
prelimsPoints: EIA is a process of evaluating the likely environmental impacts of a proposed project or development.
The EIA Notification of 2006 (and subsequent amendments) governs the process in India.
It involves screening, scoping, public consultation, appraisal, and environmental clearance.
The new guidelines aim to address concerns regarding dilution of environmental norms and lack of transparency.
mainsPoints: Discuss the objectives and importance of Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) in ensuring sustainable development in India.
Analyze the key features of the new EIA guidelines and how they aim to improve the effectiveness and transparency of the environmental clearance process.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing EIA effectively, including issues related to public participation, expert appraisal, and post-clearance compliance.
Suggest reforms to make the EIA process more robust and responsive to ecological concerns while facilitating legitimate economic development.
GS3.imageDescription: A hand holding a sapling against a backdrop of industrial development, symbolizing environmental balance.
GS3.trivia: The concept of Environmental Impact Assessment originated in the United States with the National Environmental Policy Act of 1969.

sources: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
Ministry of New and Renewable Energy
